question: Will Anthony Davis lead the NBA in rebounds during the 2025–26 NBA season?
description:
This market will resolve according to the player that finishes the 2025–26 NBA regular season with the highest rebounds per-game average of any qualified player.

In the event of a tie for the highest rebounds per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: **Nikola Jokic's statistical dominance** anchors his 93.4% implied probability as NBA rebounds per game leader, pacing the league at 13.4 rebounds per contest through 10 games amid triple-double averages and full workloads for the Nuggets. His elite positioning, outlet passing drawing defenders, and 36+ minutes nightly sustain this edge, with no injury concerns from official reports. Karl-Anthony Towns (2.9%) lags at 12.1 in fewer outings post-Knicks trade, while Domantas Sabonis (1.0%) and others face tougher paces or rotations. Realistic challenges include Jokic load management, nagging injuries, or rivals feasting on soft schedules, though his track record—three straight top-three finishes—tempers upset odds per trader consensus.

In the event of a tie for the highest assists per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: Nikola Jokic commands 96% trader consensus for NBA assists per game leader, propelled by his league-high 10.4 APG through 15 games, showcasing elite vision in Denver's fluid offense amid Jamal Murray's shooting slump that amplifies Jokic's playmaking load. His four straight seasons above 9 APG underscore reliability, with Nuggets' home-heavy schedule and health edge reinforcing the wisdom of crowds. Challengers like Cade Cunningham (9.2 APG on Detroit's fast pace) or Immanuel Quickley (8.7 APG surge post-trade) could threaten via injury absences for Jokic—such as ankle tweaks—or sustained hot streaks, though historical precedents for overtaking such gaps midseason are slim absent major disruptions.

This market will resolve according to the player that finishes the 2025–26 NBA regular season with the highest three pointers made per-game average of any qualified player.

In the event of a tie for the highest three pointers made per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: Luka Doncic's dominant 94.5% implied probability stems from his league-leading 4.9 three-pointers made per game through 22 contests, fueled by elite volume (14.2 attempts) and 34.5% efficiency on high-usage looks in Dallas' pick-and-roll heavy offense. Recent developments like his 8-of-12 outburst versus Golden State have widened his gap over pursuers like Stephen Curry (4.4 makes in 20 games) and LaMelo Ball (4.2 in 18), with traders pricing in Doncic's durability and remaining schedule edge. Realistic challenges include a Doncic injury hiatus, Curry's historical late-season surges, or Ball's hot streaks amid Charlotte's fast pace, though catch-up math favors the favorite absent major disruptions.

In the event of a tie for the highest three pointers made per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: Luka Doncic's dominant 94.5% implied probability stems from his league-leading 4.9 three-pointers made per game through 22 contests, fueled by elite volume (14.2 attempts) and 34.5% efficiency on high-usage looks in Dallas' pick-and-roll heavy offense. Recent developments like his 8-of-12 outburst versus Golden State have widened his gap over pursuers like Stephen Curry (4.4 makes in 20 games) and LaMelo Ball (4.2 in 18), with traders pricing in Doncic's durability and remaining schedule edge. Realistic challenges include a Doncic injury hiatus, Curry's historical late-season surges, or Ball's hot streaks amid Charlotte's fast pace, though catch-up math favors the favorite absent major disruptions.

In the event of a tie for the highest blocks per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: Victor Wembanyama's commanding lead in blocks per game—averaging 3.7 through 24 contests for the Spurs—anchors his 98.6% trader consensus, outpacing challengers like Chet Holmgren (2.3 bpg) and Evan Mobley (1.7 bpg) by wide margins amid his elite rim protection and 7'4" frame. Recent developments, including 5+ block outings in three of his last five games, reinforce the wisdom of crowds pricing him as near-certain, with no major injuries reported on official reports. Realistic challenges include a Wembanyama setback or extended absence, or an improbable surge from high-volume shot-blockers like Walker Kessler if minutes expand, though historical paces and remaining schedule favor the rookie phenom's dominance.

eventMetadata.context_description: Cason Wallace tops trader consensus at 59.5% implied probability for NBA steals per game leader, driven by his league-leading 3.2 steals per game through 10 outings, fueled by OKC's fast-paced defense and his 32.5 minutes nightly. Tyrese Maxey's 24.6% share reflects his 2.8 SPG average, boosted by Philadelphia's uptempo style post-Paul George addition, though turnovers slightly temper efficiency. Kawhi Leonard at 6.3% gains from elite on-ball disruption (2.1 SPG in limited action), but load management and Clippers' slower pace cap volume. Recent surges include Wallace's four-steal games versus Portland and Memphis, while Maxey's streak faces tougher schedules ahead; upsets remain possible with 70+ games left.

In the event of a tie for the highest steals per-game average, this market will resolve in favor of the player who appeared in the greater number of games. If the tied players also played the same number of games, the market will resolve in favor of the player whose listed last name comes first alphabetically.

Qualification for inclusion in official NBA leaderboards (such as minimum games or statistical thresholds) will be determined according to NBA rules and applied exactly as reflected in the official leaderboard.

The resolution source will be the NBA (NBA.com/stats).

eventMetadata.context_description: Cason Wallace tops trader consensus at 59.5% implied probability for NBA steals per game leader, driven by his league-leading 3.2 steals per game through 10 outings, fueled by OKC's fast-paced defense and his 32.5 minutes nightly. Tyrese Maxey's 24.6% share reflects his 2.8 SPG average, boosted by Philadelphia's uptempo style post-Paul George addition, though turnovers slightly temper efficiency. Kawhi Leonard at 6.3% gains from elite on-ball disruption (2.1 SPG in limited action), but load management and Clippers' slower pace cap volume. Recent surges include Wallace's four-steal games versus Portland and Memphis, while Maxey's streak faces tougher schedules ahead; upsets remain possible with 70+ games left.

question: Will the Boston Celtics finish as the #1 seed in the 2025–2026 NBA Eastern Conference?
description:
This market will resolve according to the team that finishes with the best regular-season record in the 2025–2026 NBA Eastern Conference standings.

This market will resolve to “No” if it becomes impossible for this team to finish the 2025–26 NBA Regular Season with the best Regular Season record in the Eastern Conference.

If multiple teams are tied for most wins in the 2025–26 NBA Regular Season in the Eastern Conference, this market will resolve in favor of the team that receives the number 1 seed for the 2026 NBA Playoffs. A list of the NBA’s Tiebreak Procedures can be found here: https://ak-static-int.nba.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2017/06/NBA_Tiebreaker_Procedures.pdf.

The resolution source for this market will be information from the NBA.

eventMetadata.context_description: Boston Celtics command the NBA Eastern Conference standings at 61-17, holding a 14-win lead over Milwaukee with four games left, positioning them as virtual locks for the #1 seed per official reports. Yet trader consensus prices Detroit Pistons at 82% implied probability, Boston at 13.9%, Knicks 6.7%, and Cavaliers 1.1%, possibly from low-volume outlier bets amid market illiquidity. Celtics' recent form includes wins over contenders with Jayson Tatum and Jaylen Brown healthy, while Pistons languish at 14-64 following a grueling schedule and Cade Cunningham's absence. Knicks benefit from Jalen Brunson's return but face injury depth issues; Cavs prioritize playoff rest.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in NBA win totals markets tilts toward overs for top Eastern squads like the Cavaliers and Celtics, who are pacing well above preseason projections through December, bolstered by healthy rotations and favorable remaining schedules before the All-Star break. Western frontrunners Thunder reflect similar optimism at 70%+ implied probabilities for exceeding 53.5 wins, driven by Shai Gilgeous-Alexander's MVP-caliber form and Chet Holmgren's rim protection amid minimal injuries. Key risks include lingering ailments for Embiid (76ers under pressure) and LeBron (Lakers lagging), plus the February trade deadline that could reshape contenders; upsets remain likely given the league's parity and road-heavy stretches ahead.
